title: Exodus 6
subtitle: God Renews His Promise; The Covenant Name LORD; Genealogy

sections.header: Verses 1—13 — God Renews His Covenant Promise: I Am the Lord

heb.paragraph: V.3: "I appeared to Abraham, Isaac and Jacob as God Almighty (El Shaddai), but by my name the LORD (YHWH) I did not make myself fully known to them." This is not a contradiction of earlier texts where YHWH appears — it is a statement about depth of revelation. The patriarchs knew the name; Exodus is the generation that will know the name through covenant fulfillment in action. YHWH is the covenant name, the name of redemption.

V.4-5: The word berît (covenant) anchors the entire Exodus commission: God hears Israel's groaning and "remembers" his covenant. In biblical Hebrew, "remembering" a covenant (zākar berît) means actively fulfilling its terms, not merely recalling a past agreement. The four-fold promise of v.6-8 ("I will bring out / I will free / I will redeem / I will take") is the covenant being remembered into action.

hist.historical: The divine self-revelation 'I am YHWH' (ʾănî YHWH) functions as a royal-decree formula. In the ancient Near East, a suzerain's self-identification inaugurated covenant declarations. The statement that God appeared to the patriarchs as 'El Shaddai' but not by his name YHWH has been debated since antiquity. The name YHWH is linguistically related to the Hebrew verb hāyâ ('to be'), suggesting active, dynamic presence rather than static existence.
hist.context: The seven "I will" statements (vv.6–8) are God's covenant oath in response to Moses's complaint. They structure the entire Exodus narrative: each "I will" is a divine commitment that the plagues, the crossing, and the covenant at Sinai will fulfil. The number seven signals completeness — this is the totality of God's redemptive intention for Israel. Jewish tradition reads these seven statements as the basis for four cups of wine at Passover (corresponding to vv.6–7).

refs.note: God appeared to Abraham "as God Almighty" (ʾēl šadday) — the patriarchal revelation.
YHWH was named at the bush; 6:2–3 explains the progressive nature of that revelation.
Paul's "you have received the Spirit of adoption" echoes the covenant formula "I will take you to be my people" — adoption as the form of redemption.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The statement 'by my name the LORD I did not make myself known to them' (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob) is the crux of the documentary source division: J uses YHWH throughout Genesis; E and P do not. Source critics take 6:3 as the P source's introduction of the divine name. Conservative scholars read 6:3 as a claim about experiential knowledge — the patriarchs knew the name but not its full covenant significance as the God who redeems an entire nation from superpower oppression.

The verb 'redeem' (ga'al) appears here for the first time in Exodus. It is the language of the kinsman-redeemer (go'el) who buys back family members from slavery. By using this term, God presents himself as Israel's divine kinsman — the one with both the obligation and the power to purchase his people's freedom. The Exodus is framed as a family redemption, not merely a national liberation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The renewed commission in ch.6 is notably more formal and elaborated than the burning bush encounter. Alter notes the shift in register: this passage has the structure of a covenant formulary — self-identification, historical recital, promise enumeration, concluding formula. The seven-fold 'I will' of vv.6–8 has the character of a legal covenant document. God is not merely encouraging Moses; he is formally binding himself to a programme of action.

'But they did not listen to Moses, because of their broken spirit and harsh slavery.' Alter notes the phrase qotzer ruach (shortness of spirit, crushed spirit) is unique in the OT. The slaves cannot receive the liberation because oppression has destroyed their capacity to imagine it. This is a realistic observation about the psychology of the enslaved — freedom must be enacted before it can be believed, not the reverse.

Calvin on the disclosure of the divine name: 'God had made himself known to the patriarchs under a different form — he had not yet displayed that power by which he was about to free his people. Now, therefore, he promises that he will be known by a new and unusual proof of his power.' The name YHWH is not new information but new experience — the patriarchs knew the name; what they did not know was the full saving power the name encodes. The Exodus is the name's experiential definition.

Calvin on the covenant promises: 'In these words, as in a mirror, we behold the grace of God towards his Church from the beginning. He binds himself to his people by his word; he makes them his peculiar possession; he promises to be their God.' The seven 'I wills' are Calvin's proof that the initiative in the covenant is entirely divine. God commits himself; Israel does not earn the commitment.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on El Shaddai ('God Almighty') explains the etymology is debated: possibly from Hebrew shadday ('sufficient') or Akkadian shadu ('mountain'). Most likely it emphasises divine power and provision. The note distinguishes the two forms of divine self-disclosure: El Shaddai = God as protector and provider of the patriarchal promises; YHWH = God as covenant-keeper and active redeemer. Both are true of the same God; they emphasise different aspects of the divine character.

The NET note on ga'al ('redeem') discusses the go'el institution: the nearest male relative who had both the obligation and right to redeem family property or members from slavery. By presenting himself as Israel's go'el, God is claiming kinship responsibility. The Exodus is not an act of power alone but an act of covenantal family obligation — God redeems his people because they are his people.

MacArthur: By my name the LORD I did not make myself fully known to them. The Exodus is the event of the covenant name's full experiential disclosure. The patriarchs knew the name YHWH but did not experience the redemptive content that name carries.
MacArthur: The seven I will statements are the covenant charter of the Exodus. They are entirely God's initiative -- no conditions, no qualifications. The redemption is wholly God's doing.

sections.header: Verses 14—30 — The Genealogy of Moses and Aaron

heb.paragraph: V.6: "I will redeem you with an outstretched arm." The verb gāʾal — the kinsman-redeemer root (cf. Boaz/Ruth) — is used here for God's own redemptive action. As the gōʾēl pays the price to free a relative from slavery or debt, God acts as Israel's divine kinsman to buy them out of Egypt. This becomes the paradigm for all subsequent redemption theology including Paul's in Gal 3-4.

The genealogy in Exod 6:14-25 interrupts the narrative to anchor Moses and Aaron in tribal history. Their Levitical credentials — descendants of Levi, of Kohath, of Amram — are essential. The one who will mediate the covenant must be traceable. The genealogy is not bureaucratic padding but theological preparation: the covenant people are a named, rooted community, not a crowd.

hist.historical: The genealogy of vv. 14–25 follows Egyptian and Mesopotamian king-list conventions, establishing legitimacy through lineage. Levi's sons Gershon, Kohath, and Merari correspond to the three Levitical clans that later served the tabernacle. The lifespans listed (Levi 137, Kohath 133, Amram 137) follow a pattern of idealised numbers common in ancient genealogical records. Aaron's marriage to Elisheba daughter of Amminadab connects the priestly line to the tribe of Judah.
hist.context: The seven "I will" statements (vv.6–8) are God's covenant oath in response to Moses's complaint. They structure the entire Exodus narrative: each "I will" is a divine commitment that the plagues, the crossing, and the covenant at Sinai will fulfil. The number seven signals completeness — this is the totality of God's redemptive intention for Israel. Jewish tradition reads these seven statements as the basis for four cups of wine at Passover (corresponding to vv.6–7).

refs.note: God appeared to Abraham "as God Almighty" (ʾēl šadday) — the patriarchal revelation.
YHWH was named at the bush; 6:2–3 explains the progressive nature of that revelation.
Paul's "you have received the Spirit of adoption" echoes the covenant formula "I will take you to be my people" — adoption as the form of redemption.

sarna.source: Nahum Sarna, JPS Torah Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The genealogy reaches its climax in the naming of Aaron's grandson Phinehas (v.25), whose priestly lineage Sarna identifies as the point of the entire genealogical digression. Phinehas will appear in Num 25:7–13 as the zealous priest who averts divine wrath and is rewarded with a covenant of perpetual priesthood. The genealogy traces the Aaronic line to its most celebrated heir — validating the entire priestly tradition that Exodus inaugurates.

The genealogy closes by explicitly connecting its two central figures to the narrative frame: 'It was this Aaron and Moses to whom the LORD said... it was they who spoke to Pharaoh.' Sarna notes the repetition is the genealogy's theological conclusion: the cosmic mission of the Exodus is being executed by historically specific, genealogically verifiable men.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The genealogy's placement — in the middle of Moses' renewed commission — is awkward by modern narrative standards but conventional in ancient composition. Alter notes the Wiederaufnahme (resumptive repetition) of vv.28–30 — the text essentially repeats the commissioning statement of v.10–12 after the genealogy, picking up the thread. This is a standard ancient editorial technique for handling embedded material. The genealogy validates what follows; the narrative frame resumes without apology.

The genealogy closes with a resumptive narrative device — repeating the frame that introduced it. Alter notes the effect: the genealogy is bracketed by the larger narrative, and when we return to Moses and Aaron they carry the genealogical weight of the entire Israelite lineage. They speak to Pharaoh as representatives of the whole covenant people.

Calvin on the genealogy: 'Moses inserts the genealogy, that he may show his own origin, and also that of his brother Aaron. For their authority would have had less weight if their tribe and family had not been distinguished.' The genealogy is not pedantry but credentialing — it establishes Moses and Aaron as legitimate representatives of the covenant people, not self-appointed leaders of uncertain origin.

Calvin on the genealogical placement: Moses wanted to establish the authentic lineage of the Exodus leaders so no one could doubt their authority. The genealogy is legal certification as well as historical record — it establishes that Aaron and Moses stand in the direct line of the Levitical covenant.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on Amram marrying his aunt Jochebed notes that Lev 18:12 later prohibits marriage between nephew and aunt. The note explains that the Sinai law was not yet in force — pre-Sinai patriarchal marriage customs were more permissive (cf. Abraham marrying his half-sister, Gen 20:12). The genealogical note is not inconsistency but evidence of historical distance between the patriarchal period and the Sinai legislation.

The NET note on the resumptive formula ('it was this Aaron and Moses') discusses its function as a discourse marker — signalling the end of the genealogical digression and the return to the main narrative. The note traces similar resumptive devices elsewhere in the Pentateuch.

MacArthur: Moses's renewed complaint (my lips are uncircumcised) introduces the Aaron-as-spokesman arrangement. The two-man team -- Moses hears from God, Aaron speaks to Pharaoh -- foreshadows the prophetic pattern: God speaks to the prophet who speaks to the people.

MacArthur: The genealogy focuses on Levi's line, narrowing to Aaron and Moses in v.26-27. The repetition in v.26 and v.27 (the same Aaron and Moses) frames the genealogy as a credential statement: the men who will confront Pharaoh are genealogically certified members of Israel's covenant community.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: The number seven signals completeness — this is the totality of God's redemptive intention for Israel…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationESV"I will bring you out... I will deliver you... I will redeem you... I will take you to be my people, and I will be your God... you shall know that I am the LORD your God."NIV"I will bring you out... I will free you... I will redeem you... I will take you as my own people, and I will be your God. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God."

src.title: Kinsman-Redeemer (gōʾēl) Law
src.quote: Lev 25:47–55 formalises what God does here in ch.6 — the nearest kin's obligation to buy back an enslaved family member. God acts as Israel's divine gōʾēl.
src.note: The redemption theology of Exodus 6 is grounded in Israelite kinship law.

src.title: Hittite Suzerainty Treaties (c.1400–1200 BC)
src.quote: These treaties begin with a historical prologue ("I am the one who...") followed by covenant stipulations — the structure of Exod 6:2–8 matches this form closely.
src.note: The seven covenant pledges follow the ANE treaty-formula pattern.

rec.text: The four cups of Passover Seder are traditionally linked to the four redemption verbs of Exod 6:6–7 ("bring out, deliver, redeem, take"). Jewish communities have been drinking the shape of these promises for millennia.
Uses Exod 6:3 to argue for progressive revelation — God reveals himself in stages appropriate to each era of redemptive history.

rows.label: The Covenant Name Disclosed: YHWH
rows.text: I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them. The Exodus is the moment of the covenant name's full disclosure: the character encoded in YHWH will be shown in what follows.

rows.label: The Genealogy at the Hinge: Moses and Aaron Certified
rows.text: The genealogy reaches its climax in Aaron's grandson Phinehas. The mission is entrusted to genealogically certified, historically located men -- not abstractions but persons whose lineage can be traced and verified.

hebtext.note: V.3: "I appeared to Abraham, Isaac and Jacob as God Almighty (El Shaddai), but by my name the LORD (YHWH) I did not make myself fully known to them." This is not a contradiction of earlier texts where YHWH appears — it is a statement about depth of revelation. V.4-5: The word berît (covenant) anchors the entire Exodus commission: God hears Israel's groaning and "remembers" his covenant. In biblical Hebrew, "remembering" a covenant (zākar berît) means actively fulfilling its terms, not merely recalling a past agreement. The four-fold promise of v.6-8 ("I will bring out / I will free / I will redeem / I will take") is the covenant being remembered into action.

V.6: "I will redeem you with an outstretched arm." The verb gāʾal — the kinsman-redeemer root (cf. Boaz/Ruth) — is used here for God's own redemptive action. As the gōʾēl pays the price to free a relative from slavery or debt, God acts as Israel's divine kinsman to buy them out of Egypt. The genealogy in Exod 6:14-25 interrupts the narrative to anchor Moses and Aaron in tribal history. Their Levitical credentials — descendants of Levi, of Kohath, of Amram — are essential. The one who will mediate the covenant must be traceable. The genealogy is not bureaucratic padding but theological preparation: the covenant people are a named, rooted community, not a crowd.

YHWH was named at the bush; 6:2–3 explains the progressive nature of that revelation.
Paul's "you have received the Spirit of adoption" echoes the covenant formula "I will take you to be my people" — adoption as the form of redemption.
God appeared to Abraham "as God Almighty" (ʾēl šadday) — the patriarchal revelation.
YHWH was named at the bush; 6:2–3 explains the progressive nature of that revelation.
Paul's "you have received the Spirit of adoption" echoes the covenant formula "I will take you to be my people" — adoption as the form of redemption.

tx.title: The divine name revelation — El Shaddai to YHWH
tx.content: MT states 'I appeared to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob as El Shaddai, but by my name YHWH I was not known to them.' This creates a tension with Genesis passages where the patriarchs use YHWH. The LXX translates El Shaddai as θεὸς ὢν αὐτῶν ('being their God'). SP follows MT.
tx.note: This verse is a cornerstone of the Documentary Hypothesis (P source). The textual witnesses do not resolve the tension — it is present in all versions, confirming it belongs to the earliest recoverable text.

tx.title: Genealogy of Moses and Aaron — minor variants
tx.content: The genealogy shows minor name-spelling differences between MT and LXX (e.g., Amram's wife Jochebed appears as Ιωχαβεδ in LXX). The SP agrees with MT on names. The genealogy's placement interrupts the narrative — a feature preserved in all witnesses, suggesting it was embedded early.
tx.note: The narrative disruption is a textual datum: no ancient scribe relocated the genealogy to a smoother position, despite the awkward interruption.

positions.name: Evidence for separate sources (J and P)
positions.argument: Genesis consistently uses YHWH (attributed to J), while P uses Elohim. Exodus 6:3 (attributed to P) introduces YHWH as a new name because P's author did not know of J's earlier usage. The contradiction is resolved by recognising two originally independent documents combined by later editors.

positions.name: Single authorship — qualitative disclosure
positions.argument: The patriarchs used the name YHWH (Gen 15:7; 28:13) but did not experience the redemptive acts the name encodes. Exodus 6:3 claims that the full covenantal significance of YHWH — as the God who redeems with mighty acts from national slavery — was not previously demonstrated. The 'not knowing' is experiential, not nominal.

positions.name: Single authorship — rhetorical question reading
positions.argument: The Hebrew can be read as a rhetorical question: 'And by my name YHWH, was I not known to them?' This reading removes the apparent contradiction entirely and is grammatically possible, though requiring an interrogative sense without the usual particle.
